text: Hello everybody. This is edited video of robotic look anterior resection done at Astro Hospital, Bangalore.
The patient here is a forty nine year old lady who was presented with bleeding PR since four months. Imaging revealed a proximal rectal growth with perirectal fat standing and few mesorectal nodes.
After multidisciplinary tumor board meeting, she was planned for neoadjuvant chemoradiation.

The surgeon uses the SI robotic system to perform initial dissection around the sacral promontory. The sigmoid mesocolon is lifted anteriorly and superiorly, and the dissection plane is developed between the sigmoid mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, specifically between Toldt's fascia and the retroperitoneal fascia.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the mesocolon, progressing cranially towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery and laterally towards Toldt's line, using a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon dissects soft tissue around the inferior mesenteric artery, moving towards the specimen side. The inferior mesenteric artery is visible, with the dissection plane aligned along it. The inferior mesenteric artery and the Toldt's fascia are lifted anteriorly.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly along the established plane, reaching the sacral promontory and extending laterally towards the torus fascia on the lateral abdominal wall.
The ureter and gonadal pedicle are visible, falling towards the retroperitoneum as the dissection progresses to the torus line.
The surgeon divides the infusion machine record between Hem-o-lok clips, securing the divided sections.

The surgeon dissects soft tissue lateral and cranial to the inferior mesenteric artery, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein. The vein is carefully bared and divided between hemlock clips, while preserving the nerves supplying the urinary bladder.
I would like to emphasize upon the initial laparoscopy and the parking of small hole onto the superior right quadrant without which this deception, this procedure would be difficult to perform.
The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane of the mesocolon, moving inferiorly towards the rectum between the mesorectal fascia and the pelvic fascia.

The iliac vessels and ureter are visible as the surgeon makes precise pelvic cuts. Dissection begins in the avascular plane between the mesorectal fascia and the pelvic fascia, known as the 'holy plane.'

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to retract the rectum anteriorly and superiorly, while carefully maintaining dissection within the plane to preserve the nerve bundles supplying the urinary bladder.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection until the levator ani muscles are visible. Bilateral dissection of the mesorectum is performed, revealing the levator ani muscles in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects the lateral part of the mesorectum, maintaining the established plane. Most of the bilateral mesorectum dissection is completed via the posterior route. The focus shifts to the cranial part, where the splenic flexure is mobilized.

The surgeon observes the mesocolon adjacent to the serratus fascia, noting that the spleen flexure was not completely mobilized. The initial dissection is complete, requiring only the division of the peritoneum to access the pre-dissected plane.
The surgeon makes lateral peritoneal cuts from the left lateral aspect, reaching the posterior dissection plane. Further lateral resection on the left side is completed.
The surgeon makes anterior cuts in the Douglas peritoneum between the rectum and the vagina, creating a dissection plane between these structures. A similar dissection is performed on the right side.
The assistant retracts the uterus, providing a clearer view of the surgical field.

The surgeon performs mesorectal division on the left lateral side, continuing circumferential mesorectal cuts to ensure adequate margins for oncological safety. The cuts extend approximately five centimeters beyond the distal extent of the primary tumor.

The surgeon extends the dissection towards the left half of the mesorectum, maintaining the appropriate anatomical plane. The mesorectum is visually intact and resembles the classical description similar to the buttocks.

The surgeon uses a 12mm assistant port in the suprapubic region to place an Acelon-M-M stapler, dividing the rectum. The mesorectum on the left side of the rectum is visible, and circumferential dissection is completed before firing the stapler.

The surgeon marks the proximal site of division approximately fifteen centimeters from the tumor growth. The colon is lifted anteriorly and inferiorly at this site. The mesocolon is divided cranially to the inferior mesenteric vein pedicle, with visible separation of the mesocolon tissue.

The surgeon performs a Farenstier incision of approximately four centimeters to exteriorize a section of the colon. The proximal cut is made, and an anastomosis is completed using a 29 mm circular stapler. An ileostomy is fashioned.
